title: Numbers, Age, and the Calendar
learningGoal: Learn Hindi numbers 1-10, how to state your age, ask for the time, and the days of the week.
instructions: This lesson introduces numbers and time. Hindi numbers do not follow a strict logical pattern after 10, so mastering 1-10 perfectly is critical. We will also learn how to state your age and navigate the days of the week.

lessonContent.bodyText:
Let's start with the most basic numbers. You will use these every day when buying things.

- १ : एक (Ek) - One
- २ : दो (Do) - Two
- ३ : तीन (Teen) - Three
- ४ : चार (Chaar) - Four
- ५ : पाँच (Paanch) - Five

Notice the Devanagari numerals (१, २, ३, ४, ५). While standard Western numerals (1, 2, 3) are used everywhere in modern India, you will still see Devanagari numerals on buses, coins, and official documents.

Now let's complete the base-10 set.

- ६ : छह (Chhah) - Six (Often pronounced 'chhe')
- ७ : सात (Saat) - Seven
- ८ : आठ (Aath) - Eight
- ९ : नौ (Nau) - Nine
- १० : दस (Das) - Ten

To state your age, use the word 'saal' (साल), which means 'years'.

The structure is: Main [Number] saal ka/ki hoon. 
Wait, what is 'ka/ki'? In Hindi, adjectives and postpositions change based on gender.
- If you are male, use 'ka' (का).
- If you are female, use 'ki' (की).

Male: मैं दस साल का हूँ। (Main das saal ka hoon) - I am ten years old.
Female: मैं दस साल की हूँ। (Main das saal ki hoon) - I am ten years old.

To ask about quantities, use 'Kitna' (कितना). Since Hindi has gender and plurals, 'Kitna' changes:
- Kitna (Singular masculine)
- Kitne (Plural masculine - used for counting things like days or rupees)
- Kitni (Feminine)

Example: 'Kitne?' (How many?)
Example: 'Kitne saal?' (How many years?)

To talk about time on the clock, we use the word 'Baje' (बजे), which means 'o'clock'.

- एक बजे (Ek baje) = 1 o'clock
- दो बजे (Do baje) = 2 o'clock
- तीन बजे (Teen baje) = 3 o'clock

To ask 'What time is it?', we literally ask 'How many have struck?': कितने बजे हैं? (Kitne baje hain?)

lessonContent.sectionTitle: 6. Days of the Week (Saptah ke Din)
lessonContent.bodyText:
All days of the week in Hindi end with the word 'vaar' (वार), which means day. They are named after celestial bodies.

- सोमवार (Somvaar) - Monday (Moon day)
- मंगलवार (Mangalvaar) - Tuesday (Mars day)
- बुधवार (Budhvaar) - Wednesday (Mercury day)
- गुरुवार (Guruvaar) - Thursday (Jupiter day)
- शुक्रवार (Shukravaar) - Friday (Venus day)
- शनिवार (Shanivaar) - Saturday (Saturn day)
- रविवार (Ravivaar) - Sunday (Sun day)

To say 'Today is Monday', we use 'Aaj' (Today): आज सोमवार है। (Aaj Somvaar hai.)

learningObjective: The learner will count to 10 in Hindi, state their age using basic gender agreement, ask the time, and recite the days of the week.

examples.targetText: नमस्ते। मेरी उम्र नौ साल है। आज रविवार है।
examples.transliteration: Namaste. Meri umar nau saal hai. Aaj Ravivaar hai.
examples.baseTranslation: Hello. My age is nine years. Today is Sunday.
examples.explanation: A complete sentence using numbers, the concept of age, and a day of the week.

importantRules.ruleText: When stating your age, the postposition changes based on YOUR gender, not the word 'saal'. 'Main das saal ka hoon' (male speaker). 'Main das saal ki hoon' (female speaker).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: This is your first introduction to gendered grammar in Hindi. Every noun has a gender.

importantRules.ruleText: To ask the time, 'Kitne baje hain?' uses the plural 'Kitne' (how many) and plural 'hain' (are), because you are asking 'how many hours have struck'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Even if it is 1 o'clock (singular), the question is still plural 'Kitne baje hain'.

cultureContext.contextText: In India, people often use English words for time and numbers over 100, but 1-10 and days of the week are almost always spoken in Hindi.
dontsList: Do not ask an elder their age directly as it can be considered impolite in formal settings.

summary: You have made a massive leap! You learned numbers 1-10, how to ask 'how many' (Kitne), tell time using 'baje', and navigate the calendar with the days of the week.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: If a female says 'I am 5 years old', which postposition must she use?
What does the word 'baje' mean in the context of time?
What suffix do all Hindi days of the week share?

keyTakeaways: Age uses 'saal ka/ki' based on speaker gender.
Time is asked with 'Kitne baje hain'.
Days of the week end in 'vaar'.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Basic quantification, time-telling, and calendar navigation.
importantFacts: Numbers are written differently in Devanagari script.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners can express time, age, and days using numbers 1-10.

feedbackRules.onPass: Phenomenal! You are building a very strong functional vocabulary.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review Section 3 regarding gender. Ka is for males, Ki is for females.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good progress. Memorizing numbers takes time. Practice counting on your fingers.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: एक दो तीन चार पाँच छह सात आठ नौ दस। मैं दस साल का हूँ। कितने बजे हैं? दो बजे हैं। आज सोमवार है।

hintText: Read the gender rules carefully in Section 3. They are critical for everything else in Hindi.
commonMistakes: Using 'ka' when you are a female speaker.
